text: ILE DE LA REUNION, which in 1806 was exchanged for a time for that of ILE BONAPARTE. Its form is elliptical; and, when seen from a distance, it appears to rise gradually from every side to a high-peaked point near the centre,-the Piton des Neiges,-the alt. of which is estimated at 9,450 ft. above the level of the sea. It is about 90 geog. m. in circumf., following the principal windings of the coast; its greatest diam. from NW to SE, or from Pointe des Galets to Pointe de la Table, is 45 m. Its area is estimated at 231,550 hectares, or 572,193 acres. Physical features.] This island is composed of two volcanic mountains,—the Gros-Morne, and the Volcano; the latter of which is perpetually emitting either flame or smoke. The Gros-Morne, or Salazes [alt. 7,200 ft. Monmerque], which lies towards the centre, (a) and is of the greatest dimensions, has long ceased its eruptions; but every feature of the surrounding country,-rapid rivers running deep between perpendicular ramparts, and impeded in their course by immense rocks, - numerous craters, - basaltic prisms, often disposed in regular colonnades,-various beds of lava, -and deep valleys and ravines, -all indicate the terrible physical revolutions to which it has formerly been subject. Ages, however, have passed since the N parts of B. have been freed from the ravages of subterraneous fires; its bleak surface has been converted into a fruitful soil by the action of the atmosphere, and the industry of man; and rich plantations of coffee, and immense corn-fields, now adorn plains formerly covered with liquid lava. But towards the SE, the country becomes sterile and scorched, from the great scarcity of springs, and its proximity to the volcano; and the Pays-brûlé, or 'burnt land,' (b) extending over a surface of 12,300,000 sq. toises, is one continued desert. Bounded on the E by the ocean, and rising with a lofty ascent, it is terminated on the W by the burning dome of the Volcano, or Piton de Fournaise (c). Its barren and fuliginous aspect, and the dreary solitude which reigns around, without a habitation to break the uniformity of the prospect, compose a frightful picture of desolation. Since B. was first known, the volcano has never ceased to disquiet its inhabitants; and from actual observations since 1785, it has been ascertained, that it regularly vomits lava at least twice every year; and that in nine of these eruptions, the lava has reached as far as the sea. In the line of contact of these two mountains, which runs from NW to SE, the ground rises on both sides with a gentle ascent from the sea. In this line are the Plaine des Cafres, and the Plaine des Palmistes, through which lies the only road of communication between the opposite sides of the island. The Plaine des Cafres (d) is very unequal, and is rather a number of flats rising insensibly one above the other, than a level plain. Its highest elevation is 4,800 ft. above the level of the sea, while its lowest is only 3,600 ft. It is little better than a desert. A tree is scarcely to be seen. A few tufts of shrubs are its only productions; and the more elevated places are absolutely bare, or covered only with heath. On the NE lies the Plaine des Palmistes, (e) which forms a striking contrast to the sterility of the Plaine des Cafres. It is everywhere covered with the most luxuriant verdure, and is watered by the river Seche, which traverses its surface in a serpentine direction, and receives from its sloping sides many tributary streams. This plain resembles an immense circle, surrounded on every side, except towards the ocean, by a rampart rising gradually from the centre to the height of 1,800 ft. above its base, and 2,700 ft. above the level of the sea. The E part of the island, or Partie du vent, is the most beautiful in vegetation; the W, or Partie sous le vent, is the most fertile. Near the middle of the island is a lake about halfa-mile in diameter, surrounded on every side by a sloping bank which rises nearly 600 ft. above the level of its centre. This lake is called by the inhabitants the Grand Etang. It is between 30 and 40 ft. deep; and has no other source of supply than the rains which fall in its neighbourhood. During a dry season it is sometimes completely empty: which must be occasioned entirely by evaporation, as it has no visible outlet whereby it can discharge itself. A thick cloud, in general, rests upon its surface, and the atmosphere around is always foggy and unhealthy. The rivers of B. find no resemblance in those of any other country. Instead of sheets of water flowing in a level and uniform course, and refreshing the valleys which they embellish, these are torrents, sometimes weak, sometimes strong, tumbling down the sides of the mountains through deep gulleys, which they have formed or widened during a long succession of ages; and such is the impetuosity with which they rush through their channels, that huge masses of rock are carried down in their current, which, accumulating at their junction with the ocean, form at each side of their mouths lofty and impassable ramparts. The sides of their channels, in many places, form an angle of from 45 to 70 degrees, and those of some of the principal rivers are about 600 ft. in elevation. The chief rivers are the St. Denis, Séche, Est, Mat, and Remparts. None of these are supplied by springs which issue from the earth, but are indebted entirely to the rains which fall in their neighbourhood, and to the snow and mists which continually rest upon the summits of the mountains. Of course they are all torrents in the rainy season, and often nearly dry in summer. The Mat, whose course is about 30 m., is the largest. In B. the towns, and the only appearance of cultivation—with the exception of the Basson de Salazie in the Piton des Neiges group-are confined entirely to the coast, and its neighbourhood; the interior is uninhabited, and even uninhabitable. It is divided, according to M. Bory, into eleven parishes; St. Denis, Ste. Marie, Ste. Suzanne, St. André, St. Benoit, Ste. Rose, St. Joseph, St. Pierre, St. Louis du Gaul, St. Leu, and St. Paul, each having a town of the same name. St. Denis on the N coast (D), in S lat 20° 51' 43", is the cap. of B., and the residence of the governor, the supreme council, and other public functionaries. The island has no safe roadsted, nor any good harbour. Climate. The climate of B. varies greatly in different situations; but may be pronounced generally agreeable and salubrious. At an elevation of 115 ft., the maximum medium temp. of the year was found to be 28°.13 Centig., or 82°.63 F.; the minimum meexposed to all the inconveniences of tropical heat, those on the more elevated regions enjoy the richness | BOURBON. and coolness of the temperate zones; and in the Plaine des Cafres the cold is so intense that it is very dan gerous to enter upon it when in the least degree over heated: an icy and sudden wind from the neighbouring mountains, which during winter are covered with snow, may here in a moment prove fatal. Nowhere, however, is the climate so hot as might be expected from the latitude of the island. The air is pleasant and wholesome; and the inhabitants, in general, live to a very great age. The annual rate of mortality is 2.38 per cent., or 1 in 42; while in France it is 1 in 39. The country is refreshed by continual breezes from the mountains; and the snow, which accumulates during winter upon these heights, furnishes during summer a plentiful supply of water to the parched herbage of the plains. But B. owes its salubrity, in a great measure, to the hurricanes which purify and cleanse the atmosphere of every deleterious and noxious ingredient. In general, one or two of these occur every year, but they seldom exceed two days in continuance. They happen between the months of December and March; and it has been observed by the inhabitants that the want of these periodical storms is invariably succeeded by unwholesome seasons. Their approach is prognosticated by a frightful hollow noise in the mountains, accompanied by a dead calm both at sea and on shore; and the night preceding the storm, the moon appears of a crimson colour. E winds-varying from ESE to SSE-prevail for about 300 days in the year. The other winds are chiefly from the W, and occur at new and full moon. Soil and Productions. The soil is nowhere of any great depth, there being immediately underneath a black burnt rock resembling the Pays Brulé: but though not deep, it is wonderfully fruitful, producing corn, coffee, sugar, cotton, and cloves, in great abundance. In 1763, upon an extent of 125,909 acres of cultivated land, there was gathered as much cassava as was necessary to the support of the slaves; 1,135,000 lbs. of corn, 844,100 lbs. of rice, 2,879,100 lbs. of maize, and 2,535,100 lbs. of coffee, which last was bought up by the French East India company, at about 3d. per lb. But this abundance is owing more to the fertility of the soil than the industry of the colonist, whose system of agriculture, though superior to that of the Mauritius, and the neighbouring islands, would still admit of great improvements. The plantations of cotton of a superior quality might be greatly extended; and, indeed, the quantity of produce of every description might be considerably increased. The coffee-plant, now cultivated in B., was originally brought from Yemen in Arabia, in 1718. The clove-tree was first introduced into this island by M. Poivre in 1772; but it has never been brought to equal that of the Moluccas or Amboyna. It is, however, a considerable article of commerce, and, in some years, will produce 150,000 lbs. weight; but, at other times, it will scarcely exceed a third of that quantity. The surface in 1836 was distributed as follows:- 65,702 hectares. Cultivated lands, . . . . . . . 14,040 Pasturage, Woods and forests, 55,921 Waste lands, . . 95,887 . . . . 231,550 Of the 65,702 hect, of cultivated land, the distribution was as follows:- In grain crops, &c., including 23,587 hect. of maize, B. produces also white pepper, gum-benjamin, aloes, and tobacco; and the honey of St. Pierre, called miel vert, passes for the best in the world. The island abounds with palm and other kinds of wood, many of which yield odoriferous gums and resins; and its fruits are guavas, bananas, oranges, citrons, and tamarinds. The botany of this island is interesting. The Corypha umbraculifera, or cabbage-palm, attains a prodigious height. The Bambusa alpina—which is never found at a less height above sea-level than 3,800 ft.-forms a remarkable border of vegetation all round the island, except where the lava has interrupted vegetation. The Mimosa, Bombax pentandrum, Cycas circinalis, Casuarina equisetifolia, and Hernandia ovigera, or white wood, are amongst its forest trees. When this island was first discovered, it was destitute of every species of frugiverous or ravenous quadrupeds; but its rivers were well-stocked with fish; its coast with land and sea tortoises; and its woods with paroquets, pigeons, turtle-doves, and a variety of birds. The dronte, or dodo, is represented by some travellers as a native of B.; but this singular bird, with many others described by the first navigators, has now become extinct in the island, having been destroyed by its early inhabitants, many of whom lived entirely by fishing and the chase. Few of the present animal productions of B. are indigenous. Horses, oxen, hogs, and goats, were first imported by the Portuguese, and they multiplied so rapidly that when the French visited the place about a century afterwards, in 1653, they were found wandering in bands through the woods; but since the island was colonised, they have as rapidly decreased. The horses have been reduced to servitude, the oxen domesticated, the hogs destroyed; a few goats only have escaped the spear of the hunter, by retiring to the most inaccessible recesses of the mountains. No venomous creature is found in the island, and only two which are disagreeable to the sight,—these are spiders of the size of a pigeon's egg; and very large bats, which are not only skinned and eaten, but are esteemed a great delicacy. On the shores are found ambergris and coral. Commerce.] The commerce of B., except the petty traffic carried on with the Americans, consisting chiefly in the exchange of provisions for hard money and lumber, was long confined to Madagascar, the Comoro islands, and the Arab settlements on the E coast of Africa. This trade consisted in the barter of prize goods, spirits, fire-arms, and ammunition, for black cattle, rice, gold dust, elephants' teeth, and slaves. B. may be called, however, the granary of the Mauritius, as it supplies that island with one half of its provisions. Its commerce has rapidly increased of late years. In 1825, its exports to France were estimated at 7,500,000 francs, and its imports from France at 6,000,000 francs. In 1836, the importations into France from B. were— Materials of productive industry, . . . 433,716 fr. 15,087,953 Natural objects of consumption, . Manufactured ditto, 562,588 . . 16,134,257 The quantities and value of the principal articles of export, in 1836, were- Francs. Kilog. Raw sugar, 18,173,092 12,721,164 . Coffee, . BOURBON. Population.] The pop. of this island, ever since it became a French colony, has been annually increasing. In 1717, it was computed at 2,000, of whom 900 were free, and 1,100 slaves; and, in 1763, it had increased to 4,000 whites, and 15,000 slaves. In 1789 it amounted to 61,200, of whom 10,000 were whites and mulattoes, 1,200 free blacks, and 50,000 slaves; and, according to a computation, in 1810, it consisted of 90,346, of whom 16,400 were whites and creoles, 3,496 free blacks, and 70,450 slaves. In 1836, the pop. amounted to 109,330, of whom 60,513 were slaves, and 39,817 free blacks. The usual distinction of whites and blacks, however, fails here: for M. de la Barbinais assures us, that he saw in a church one family, consisting of five generations of all complexions. The eldest was a female 108 years of age, of a brown black, like the Indians at Madagascar; her daughter a mulatto; her grand-daughter a mestizo; her great-grand-daughter of a dusky yellow; her daughter, again, of an olive; and the daughter of this last, as fair as any English girl of the same age. The inhabitants of B. differ considerably in character and disposition from those of any other colony. Instead of being a motley group of strangers from every country, who come merely in search of wealth, and hasten back to the land of their infancy, the Bourbonese are natives of the soil, and inherit from father to son the patrimony of their ancestors. Hence, they have become so attached to their country that they have acquired, in a strong degree, what the French call esprit de province. Property is not here continually changing its masters. It will remain for ages in the same family, and its possessors live and die in the land of their fathers. The people are, in general, of a gentle and industrious disposition; and are distinguished for their hospitality and simplicity of manners. They marry at an early age, and are remarkable for their attention to domestic duties, and their attachment to their husbands and children. "Both men and women," says Admiral Kempenfelt, "are strong and well-made, breathe a wholesome air, are in continual exercise, and are distinguished for moderation and temperance." Their houses are chiefly built of wood, and are cool and agreeable. Government.] The government of B. under the late regime was administered by a governor, assisted by a privy council composed of the three chief officers of administration, and 2 councillors selected from the inhabitants, and a colonial council of 30 members, who were elected for 5 years by all domiciliated Frenchmen of 25 years of age, and possessing property in the island to the extent of 20,000 francs. The number of electors in 1837 was 1,145. The administrative subdivision of this island is into 2 arrondissements, 6 cantons, and 13 communes or parishes. The garrison troops in 1837 amounted to 814 men; the militia to 6,593. The revenue in 1837 amounted to 2,149,563 francs; and the expenditure, exclusive of 782,865 francs charged against the mothercountry, equalled the receipts.—The number of pupils in the different schools throughout the island in 1837 was only 2,316. History.] The isle of B. was first discovered in 1545, by Don Pedro Mascarenhas, a Portuguese navigator, who gave it his own name. From that time it was occasionally visited by the Spaniards and Portuguese, for refreshing their crews and getting water, till 1642, when Pronis, the French commandant at Madagascar, took possession of it in the name of his sovereign, and sent thither twelve malefactors who had been condemned to perpetual exile. From their description of the country, M. de Flacourt, a director sion to Madagascar, was induced to form a settlement upon its shores. In 1654, he sent over 8 French and 6 Negroes as its first colony, and changed its name into Bourbon. But nothing could be expected from this miserable establishment. Unacquainted with the climate, they lost their first crop by a hurricane; and tiring of their solitary situation, and receiving no succour from Madagascar, they quitted the island, and embarked with all their property in an English vessel for Madras. B. now became the favourite resort of pirates and buccaneers. When the French were driven from Madagascar by the natives, who surprised Fort Dauphine, and massacred every white that fell into their hands, those that escaped took refuge in B., where they found, at a small expense of labour, tranquillity and plenty. These, with the crew of a privateer which had been wrecked upon the coast, and some Indian women that were on board, formed a considerable settlement. Having planted aloes, tobacco, and various kinds of roots, they carried on a small trade with any ships that anchored in their roads for refreshment; and even built some small vessels of their own, in which they made a trip to Madagascar to purchase slaves, which they employed in their plantations. The discovery of coffee-trees growing wild, led to the cultivation of that article. The new colony increasing in numbers and in wealth, when the French East India company, assuming the property of the island, sent thither, in 1711, six families and a governor, and established a provincial council dependant upon that of Pondicherry. In 1712, the Mauritius, or Isle of France, having been abandoned by the Dutch, was occupied by settlers from B.; and so rapid was the progress of the new colony that in twenty years it became the seat-ofgovernment. Licentiousness, confusion, and anarchy, were the characteristics of B. until 1735, when M. de la Bourdennaye was appointed to the supreme command of the Isle of France and B. Under his government, the island soon began to assume another appearance. New forts and batteries were raised, and so wellsupplied with cannon, military and naval stores, and every other mean of defence, that when Admiral Boscawen appeared before these islands in 1748, he found them so completely fortified that he was obliged, after some fruitless cannonading, to pursue his voyage. In 1767 B., with the Mauritius, was ceded back to the Crown. Upon the breaking out of the Revolution, these islands resisted with firmness the execution of the decree issued by the Directory for the immediate abolition of slavery throughout all their colonies. They took the oath of allegiance to the new government in the mother-country, but would not listen to the emancipation of their Negroes; and the agents sent to regulate affairs by the executive in France were compelled to return home. A colonial assembly was elected, the governor deposed, and B., and the Isle of France, continued virtually independent till 1799. An expedition, under the command of Colonel Keating, and Commodore Rowley, sailed from India in June 1810 for the reduction of B. After a gallant defence by Colonel Susanne, the French commandant, the object of the expedition was accomplished on the 8th of July, when this island surrendered to the British arms; and paved the way for the conquest of Mauritius, the last and most important colony of France. At the general peace, B. was restored, but the Mauritius retained, by England.—Bory de St. Vincent, Voyage dans les Quatre principales Iles des Mers d'Afrique. -Viscount de Vaux's History of Mauritius and the neighbouring Islands.—A Pamphlet by an Officer of the late Expedition against Bourbon.—Notices Statistiques sur les colonies Françaises. -Thomas, Essai statistique sur l'isle B. 1838. 2 vols., 8vo.

BOURBON-LANCY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, arrond. of Charolles.—The cant., comprising 11 com., had a pop. of 9,559 in 1831.—The com. and town are 30 m. WNW of Charolles. Pop. 2,848. There are 45 saline hot springs here.

BOURBON-L'ARCHAMBAULT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Allier, arrond. of Moulins. The cant., comprising 8 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 10,874.—The com. is 18 m. W of Moulins. Pop. 2,902. The town is frequented on account of its hot saline springs. It was in ancient times the cap. of the duchy of B., from which the late royal family of France took their name. It is supposed to have been the Aqua Bormonis or Borvonis of the Roman itineraries. Pepin laid siege to it and took it in 759. The remains of the ancient castle of the lords of B. still exist here. During the Revolution of 1789 the name of this town was changed to Burges-les-Bains.

BOURBON-VENDE'E, an arrondissement, canton, and commune of France, in the dep. of La Vendee.—The arrond. comprises the 10 cant. of B., Les Essaits, Chantonnay, Saint-Fulgent, Les Herbiers, Mareuil, Montaigu, Mortagne-sur-Sevre, Poiré-sur- B., and Rocheservière. Area 257,058 hect. Pop. in 1836, 120,777; in 1846, 135,554.—The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. in 1831, 18,570.—The town is situated upon the Yon, in N lat. 46° 41' 16", 268 m. SW of Paris. Pop. in 1836, 5,257; in 1846, 5,680. It was founded by Napoleon in 1811; and is "exactly what one might expect from the hasty and arbitrary manner of its creation. A huge oblong Place forms | principal church is a fine edifice of the mixed Gothic BOU the centre and principal part of it. From the sides and corners of this, eight or ten streets branch off at right angles." All the buildings are meagre in aspect, and the barracks are beginning to fall into ruins. The surrounding district is a barren open heath. Its founder bestowed on it the name of NAPOLEON-VENDEE.

BOURBONNAIS, an ancient district of France, now forming the dep. of Allier, and the arrond. of Saint-Amand in the dep. of Cher. It was bounded on the N by Berry and Nivernois; on the E by Bourgogne and Lyonnais; on the S by Auvergne and a part of Marche; and on the W by Berry. It was successively comprised in the kingdoms of Orleans, Austrasia, and Aquitain. In 1527, on the forfeiture of Charles de Montpensier, duke of Bourbon, the duchy became united to the Crown.

BOURBONNE - LES - BAINS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haute- Marne, arrond. of Langres.—The cant., comprising 16 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 13,717.—The town, situated at the confluence of the Borne and Apance, 22 m. ENE of Langres, has a pop. of 3,272. It is celebrated for its hot saline springs, of a temp. of from 122° to 149° Fahr., and has a large bathingestablishment, and a military hospital with 540 beds. It occupies an elevated situation, estimated at 273 metres, or 895 ft. above sea-level.
BOURBOULE (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, com. of Murat-le-Quaire, on the Dordogne. Alt. 2,800 ft. above sea-level.

BOURBOURG, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, arrond. of Dunkerque.-The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,771.— The town is 12 m. SW of Dunkerque, on a canal which joins the Aa. It has manufactures of soap and of beet-root sugar, tan-works, brick-works, and rape-seed oil mills.
BOURBOURG - CAMPAGNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Bourbourg. Pop. 2,040.
BOURBRIAC, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, arrond. of Guingamp. —The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,199. —The com. is 6 m. SSW of Guingamp. Pop. 3,613.
BOURDEAUX. See BORDEAUX.
BOURDEAUX, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Drome, arrond. of Die.-The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 4,219.—The com. is 18 m. SW of Die, on the Reubion. Pop. 1,281.
BOURDEILLES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Brantome, on the l. bank of the Dronne. Pop. 1,638.
BOURDONNAY, a commune of France in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Vic. Pop. 1,026.
BOURESSE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vienne, cant. of Lussac. Pop. 1,145.

BOURG, or Bourg-en-Bresse, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of l'Ain.—The arrond. comprises 10 cant., viz., Bage-le-Chatel, B., Ceyzeriat, Coligny, Montrevel, Pont d'Ain, Pont-de-Vaux, Pont-de-Veyle, Treffort, Saint-Trivier-de-Courtes. Area 177,184 hect. Pop. in 1836, 117,753; in 1846, 124,005.—The cant., comprising 14 com., had a pop. of 18,481 in 1831.—The town is 22 m. ESE of Macon, in N lat. 46° 12' 22", E long. 5° 14'; at an alt. of 728 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1789, 8,788; in 1836, 9,528; in 1846, 10,308. It possesses a communal college, and normal school, a botanical garden, and a public library of 19,000 vols. Its manufactures are unimportant; but it carries on some trade in corn, skins, and cattle; and the district is celebrated for poultry and for honey. B. was an episcopal city from 1521 to 1536. Its and Renaissance styles.—The astronomer Lalande was born at B. on 11th July, 1732.
BOURG (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of La Capelle-Marival. Pop. 621.
BOURG (LE). See BURGTHAL.
BOURG-SOUS-BOURBON (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Bourbon- Vendee. Pop. 1,768.

BOURG-SUR-GIRONDE, or BOURG-SUR-MER, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, arrond. of Blaye.-The cant. comprises 16 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 13,247.—The town is situated on the r. bank of the Dordogne, near the junction of that river with the Gironde, 2 m. from the Bec d'Ambez, and 7 m. SSE of Blaye. Pop. 2,306. It has a small port from which 10,722 quintals of wine were exported in 1840. Its chief exports are wine and grain—the produce of the locality. In the environs is an extensive quarry of bastard marble.

BOURG-LES-VALENCE (LE), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Drome, on the r. bank of the Rhone, in the cant. and less than 1 m. to the N of Valence—of which it forms a suburb, and with which it is connected by a long street. Pop. 2,820. It is ill built; but contains considerable remains of its ancient origin: amongst these is a church built in the reign of Charlemagne.

BOURGACHARD, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Routot, 15 m. E of Pont-Audemer. Pop. 1,216. It has a wellfrequented market. In the environs, at the castle of Authonne, are a fine agricultural establishment, nursery - gardens, and a breeding establishment of horses, and of sheep of the merino species.

BOURGANEUF, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Creuse. -The arrond. comprises an area of 90,560 hect., and contains the cant. of Bénévent, Bourgneuf, Pontarion, and Royere. Pop. in 1831, 37,965; in 1836, 39,795; in 1846, 42,343.—The cant. comprises 13 com., and in 1831 possessed a pop. of 11,681.—The town is pleasantly situated on the Thorion, 18 m. SSW of Guéret. Pop. in 1789, 2,418; in 1836, 2,940; in 1846, 3,159. It has manufactories of porcelain and hemp-cloth, tile-works and a papermill. Here is a massive tower, in which Zizim, brother of the Turkish emperor Bajazet II., was confined for several years in the 15th cent.

BOURG-ARGENTAL, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Loire, arrond of St. Etienne.—The cant. comprises 9 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 8,713.—The town is 15 m. SE of St. Etienne, and 34 m. SE of Montbrison. Pop. 2,502. It contains manufactories of lace, ribbons, and crape, and cotton spinning and paper mills. The environs produce excellent silk, and present extensive nursery-gardens. Lead in small quantities has been mined here. B. has suffered much from the devastations of religious wars, famine, and pestilence.
BOURG-BARRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Rennes. Pop. 1,180.
BOURG-BLANC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finisterre, cant. of Plabennec. Pop. 1,729.
BOURG-BRUCHE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vosges, cant. of Saales. Pop. 1,257.
BOURG-D'ARLANE (LE), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, com. and half-a-mile N of Arlanc, on the Dolora. Pop. 595.
BOURG-DES-COMPTES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Guichen. Pop. 1,714.
BOURG-DE-THISY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Thisy. Pop. 1,740. BOU

BOURG-DE-VISA, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Tarn-et-Garonne, arrond. of Moissac. The cant. comprises 7 comand contained in 1831 a pop. of 6,675. The town is 15 m. NNW of Moissac. Pop. 1,018.
BOURG-D'HEM, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Creuse, cant. of Bonnat. Pop. 1,023.
BOURG-DIEU. See DE'OLS.
BOURG D'IRE', a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Segré. Pop. 1,526.

BOURG - D'OISANS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Isere, arrond. of Grenoble.—The cant. comprises 21 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 16,262.—The town is 21 m. SE of Grenoble, in a valley on the r. bank of the Romanche. Pop. 3,052. It possesses a manufactory of calico, and has an annual horse-fair. The environs contain mines of different ores, particularly of lead. A large proportion of the inhabitants become in winter itinerant venders of small-ware. The Uceni were the ancient inhabitants of the mountains which surround this town.
BOURG-DUN (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lower Seine, cant. of Offranville, 12 m. WSW of Dieppe. Pop. 981.

BOURG-DU-PE'AGE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Drome, arrond. of Valence.—The cant. comprises 13 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 20,057.—The town is on the l. bank of the Rhone, opposite Saint-Romans, and 13 m. NE of Valence. Pop. 3,577. It possesses manufactories of oil, hats, and a variety of articles of silk-tissue, also silk-mills, roperies, and tanneries. The mulberry is extensively cultivated in the environs.—B., formerly called Péage-Pisançon, derived its name and origin from a bridge built over the Isere in the 12th cent., on which a toll was levied.

BOURGES, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Cher.—The arrond. comprises an area of 246,229 hect. Pop. in 1831, 97,537; in 1836, 108,476; in 1846, 115,750. It contains 10 cant., Aix-d'Angillon, Baugy, B., Charost, Graçay, Levet, Lury, Saint-Martin-d'Auxigny, Mehun-sur-Yevre, and Vierzon.—The cant. contains the com. of B. only. The town is situated in an extensive plain, at the confluence of the Auron and Yevre, 18 m. SW of Sancerre, and 100 m. S of Paris, in a level country, but at an alt. of 528 ft. above sea-level; in N lat. 47° 4' 59", and E long. 2° 24'. Pop. in 1789, 22,906; in 1836, 25,324; in 1846, 21,670. B. consists of an old and new town. The houses are generally of a very inferior order, but several of the public buildings are extremely handsome. Of these the principal are the cathedral, a Gothic edifice of the 9th cent., and reckoned one of the finest in France; the townhouse, formerly the private residence of Jacques Cour, the unfortunate minister of Charles VII.; the archiepiscopal palace, with a public garden, in which is an obelisk raised to the memory of Bethune- Charost; and the hall of the justiciary court. B. has also a university-academy, a royal college with a museum, theological and normal schools, a public library, a house-of-refuge, and a theatre. Of the great ancient tower which defended the town, and the walls of which were upwards of 18 ft. in thickness, only the ruins now exist. In the environs are pleasant promenades, a fine nursery-garden, and two mineral springs, and a recently discovered mine of zinc. The barracks are in the outskirts of the town. B. has manufactories of cutlery, cloth, woollen coverlets, hosiery, calico, some iron-smelting works, and five printing establishments. Its commerce, which is considerable, consists in grain, cattle, sheep, wool, skins, wine, fruit, hemp, &c. Fairs are held nine PERSONAL PROPERTY AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND times a-year; and sheep markets weekly .- This town, the ancient Avaricum, the cap. of the Bituriges, was, in the year 52 B. C., taken by Cæsar, and reduced to ashes. Under Augustus it became the metropolis of Aquitania. It was ravaged by the Visigoths. In 583 it was again destroyed by Chilperic; but under Charlemagne and Philip-Augustus, it was once more restored, and became the cap. of Berri. Several councils were held in B.; and the acceptance of the Pragmatic-sanction by the clergy in 1483, took place here. It was the birth-place of Louis XI., and of the great pulpit-orator Bourdaloue.

BOURGET (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine, cant. of Pantin, 6 m. NNE of Paris. Pop. 575. It possesses large manufactories of maccaroni, and of linen and wax-cloth.-Also a town of Savoy, in the prov. of Savoy Proper, near the S extremity of the lake of the same name, 7 m. NNW of Chambery, at an alt. of 748 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 2,000. It possesses a small port, and has a manufactory of earthenware. This town was formerly one of importance, and is supposed to have been of Roman origin. Amongst other ancient remains, there still exists in it a tomb of the era of Antoninus. To the N, on the lake, is a Cistercian abbey, founded in 1125, which contains the tombs of the counts and dukes of Savoy. In its vicinity is an intermitting fountain.-The lake of Le B. is 12 m. in length, and nearly 2 m. in average breadth. It is connected, at its NW extremity, with the Rhone, by the Canal de Savières, a narrow winding channel, but sufficiently deep to carry pretty large steamers, which ply between Aix on the lake, and Lyons on the Rhone, and during the spring inundations of that river it overspreads the adjacent prairies of Aix. On the W the basin of the lake is bounded by the Mont du Chat. Its waters are extremely limpid, and abound with fish, of which the lavaret or Corregonus maræna is particularly celebrated.
BOURGHAS. See BURGHAS.
BOURGHELLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Cisoing. Pop. 1,086.
BOURG L'ABBE', a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. and com. of Caen. Pop. 4,000.
BOURG-LA-REINE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Seine, cant. of Sceaux, 4 m. SW of Paris. Pop. 997. A great cattle-market for the supply of Paris is held here every Monday.

BOURG-LAZTIC, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, arrond. of Clermont.—The cant. comprises 5 com. Pop. in 1831, 6,957.—The com. is 30 m. WSW of Clermont. Pop. 2,707. Iron is mined here.
BOURG-LE-ROI, a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of Saint-Peter, 8 m. SE of Alencon. Pop. 580.
BOURG-MADAME, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pyrenees-Orientales, cant. of Saillagouse, on the Tet. Pop. 121.
BOURGNEUF (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Loiron. Pop. 1,929. -Also a hamlet in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, com. and contiguous to the village of Touches. Pop. 597.

BOURGNEUF-EN-RETZ, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, arrond. of Paimbœuf.—The cant. comprises 5 com., and in 1837 contained a pop. of 7,597.—The town is situated on the NE side of the bay of the same name, 19 m. SSE of Paimbœuf, and 24 m. SW of Nantes. Pop. 2,680. It has a small port, accessible at high tide only, but possessing an active commerce in salt, cattle, wine, brandy, and grain. The district contains extensive saline marshes. The shores abound with excellent oysters, and the fisheries of the adjacent sea are extremely productive.—The bay of B. BOU extending from Cheveche point on the NW, to the island of Nourmoutier on the S, is 12 m. in depth, and 6 m. in breadth. It is extremely shallow, and filled with sand-banks; the greater part of its coast is lined with bristling rocks, and it is destitute of shelter on the NW. It contains the island of Bonin, formed by the Falleron, which flows into the bay near its SE extremity.
BOURGOGNE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Marne, arrond. of Rheims .- The cant. comprises 25 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 15,956.—The com. is 9 m. N of Rheims. Pop. 904.
BOURGOGNE. See BURGUNDY.

BOURGOIN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Isère, arrond. of La Tour-du- Pin.—The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 19,692.—The com. and town are situated on the Bourbre, 8 m. W of La Tour-du-Pin. Pop. 3,762. Chintz, calicoes, and beet-root sugar are manufactured here; and the place has an active trade in hemp and wool.
BOURGON, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Loiron, near the Vilaine. Pop. 1,226.

BOURG-SAINT-ANDE'OL (LE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, on the r. bank of the Rhone .- The cant., comprising 9 com., had a pop. of 1,570 in 1831.—The com. and town are 30 m. S of Privas. Silk and wine are extensively produced in this district.
BOURG-SAINT-BERNARD, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, cant. of Lanta. Pop. 1,237.

BOURGTHEROULDE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Eure, arrond. of Pont-Audemer.—The cant., comprising 24 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 10,598.—The com., 18 m. ESE of Pont- Audemer, has a pop. of 740.
BOURGUEBUS, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados, arrond. of Caen.— The cant., comprising 25 com., had a pop. of 8,962 in 1831.—The com. is 6 m. SSE of Caen. Pop. 280.

BOURGUEIL, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, arrond. of Chinon.—The cant., comprising 6 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 17,097.—The com. and town are situated on the Doit, 8 m. NNW of Chinon. Good red wine is extensively grown in the district, also lint, anise, and coriander seeds.
BOURGUIGNON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. of Pont-de-Roide. Pop. 429. There are iron-furnaces here.
BOURLERS, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. and 39 m. S of Charleroi. Pop. 535. Iron is mined here.
BOURLON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Marquion. Pop. 1,503.
BOURLOS. See Burlos.

BOURMONT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, arrond. of Chaumont. - The cant., comprising 26 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 11,092.—The com. and town are on the r. bank of the Meuse, 26 m. ENE of Chaumont. Pop. 1,118. B. has some trade in grain, wood, wine, and iron.
BOURN, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 2 m. SE by S of Caxton. Area 4,073 acres. Pop. 909.
BOURNABUT. See BURNABUT.

BOURNE, a parish and town in Lincolnshire, 35 m. SSE of Lincoln. Area 8,190 acres. Pop. of p. 3,361. It comprises the hamlets of Cawthorpe and Dyke. The town consists chiefly of one street, above a mile in length. It has some trade in wool and malt; and is connected with Boston by a canal for boats of 10 tons burden. It is one of the pollingplaces for the members for the parts of Kesteven. The B. poor-law union comprehends 37 parishes, embracing an area of 84,942 acres. Pop. in 1831, 17,174; in 1841, 19,832. Expenditure in 1838, £4,074; in 1842, £5,254; in 1846, £6,209.

BOURNE, a tributary of the Wiltshire Avon, which it joins below Salisbury, after a course of 23 m.- Also a river of France, an affluent of the Isère, which it joins near Saint-Nazaire, in the dep. of Drome, after a course of 34 m.
BOURNE (St. MARY), a parish in Southamptonshire, 3 m. NW of Whitchurch. Area 6,640 acres. Pop. 1,152.
BOURNE (West), a parish in Sussex, 7 m. WNW of Chichester. Area 4,230 acres. Pop. 2,093.
BOURNEVILLE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Quillebœuf. Pop. 840.
BOURNEZEAU, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of La Vendée, cant. of Chamouny. Pop. 1,674.
BOURNOU. See BORNU.
BOURO. See BURU.
BOURRE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Montrichard, on the r. bank of the Cher. Pop. 700.
BOURRE-BOURRE, a river of British Guayana, an affluent of the Siparuny. It is supposed to have its source at the W angle of the Parima mountains, and flows SSW.
BOURRET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Tarn-et-Garonne, cant. of Verdun-sur-Garonne. Pop. 1,209.
BOURSAULT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Marne, cant. of Dormans, 6 m. WNW of Epernay. Pop. 600.
BOURSEUL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, cant. of Plancoët. Pop. 1,368.

BOURTANGE, or BOURTANG, a town and fort of Holland, in the prov. of Groningen, arrond. and 12 m. SSE of Winschotten, on the Hanoverian frontier, in the midst of a vast marsh known as the Bourtangermeer, which lies between the W bank of the Ems and the E frontiers of Drenthe and Groningen. Pop. of district, 1,850.
BOURTH, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Verneuil. Pop. 1,667. It has iron-works, and a large pin-manufactory.
BOURTHES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Hucqueliers. Pop. 1,099.
BOURTIE, a parish in Aberdeenshire, in the Garioch district. Area 4,000 Scotch acres. Pop. in 1801, 445; in 1841, 469.

BOURTON, a tything in the p. of Shrivenham, Berks, 7 m. SW by S of Faringdon. Pop. 396.— Also a hamlet in the p. and 1 m. E of Buckingham, Bucks. Area 1,360 acres. Pop. 48.—Also a chapelry in the p. of Gillingham, Dorset, 21 m. SW of Mere. Pop. 901.—Also a township in Much-Wenlock p., in Salop. Pop. 181.—Also a p. in Somersetshire, 5\frac{1}{2} m. WSW of Bristol. Pop. 232.—Also a tything in the p. of Bishop's Cannings, Wilts. Pop. 445.
BOURTON-UPON-DUNSMOOR, a parish in Warwickshire, 4 m. W by S of Dunchurch. Area 2,520 acres. Pop. with the hamlet of Draycote, 390.
BOURTON (GREAT and LITTLE), a township in Oxfordshire, in the p. of Cropredy, 3 m. N of Panbury. Area 1,380 acres. Pop. 593.
BOURTON-ON-THE-HILL, a parish in Gloucestershire, 2 m. W of Moreton-in-the-Marsh. Area 2,960 acres. Pop. 542.
BOURTON - ON - THE - WATER, a parish in Gloucestershire, 4 m. SSW of Stow-on-the-Wold. Area 2,290 acres. Pop. 943. BOU
BOURTONHOLD, a hamlet in the p. of Buckingham, Bucks. Area 20 acres. Pop. 614.
BOUSBECQUES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Tourcoing. Pop. 1,937.
BOUSCAT (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Bordeaux. Pop. 1,729.
BOUSCAUT (LE), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Gironde, com. of Cadaujac. Pop. 180.
BOUSCHIR. See ABUSIR.
BOUSH, a town of Central Egypt, near the L bank of the Nile, 7 m. NNE of Benisuef.

BOUSSA, an interior district of Western Africa, a tributary state of the kingdom of Borgu. It is intersected by the Quorra or Niger, along which it appears to stretch 50 m. The soil is fertile; and the country is reported to be populous. The language is the same as that of the other states of Borgu, which appears to be a dialect of the Youriba; but the Cambrie Negroes, who inhabit villages in the woods near the Quorra, speak a dialect of their own .- The town of B. is situated on an island formed by the Quorra in N lat. 10° 14', and E long. 6° 11'. The river is here divided into three branches; and the city is built on an island which extends about 3 m. from N to S, and is 11 m. in breadth. The houses are built in clusters of huts, not occupying above one-tenth of the walled area. Outside the walls, there are several villages, with plantations of corn, yams, and cotton; but the whole number of inhabitants is estimated at less than 12,000 souls.—It was here that our enterprising countryman, Park, closed his career, in 1805, in the 35th year of his age. The circumstances attending the lamented fate of this enterprising traveller, as given to Captain Clapperton at Kulfu by an eye-witness, together with all the information he could collect, tallies with the storydisbelieved at the time-which Isaaco brought back from Amadi Fatuma. He said, that when the boat in which Park, after having penetrated beyond Timbuctu, was descending the Niger, came down the river, it happened unfortunately that the Fellatahs had for the first time risen in arms, and were ravaging Guber and Zamfra; that the sultan of B., on hearing that the persons in the boat were white men, and that the boat was different from any that had ever been seen before, as she had a house at one end, called his people together from the neighbouring towns, attacked and killed them, not doubting that they were the advanced guard of the Fellatah army then ravaging Sudan, under Malem Danfodio; that one of the white men was a tall man with long hair; that the number of persons in the boat was only four, two white men and two blacks; that they found great treasure in the boat; but that the people had all died who ate of the meat that was found in her. The sultan of B., who was "a little boy when the event happened," showed uneasiness when inquiries were made upon the subject; as did all his people, although, upon every other, they were as frank and communicative as they were kind and hospitable. Lander afterwards received the following additional information from a mallam or priest, whom he met with at Wawa, and who tendered it spontaneously. "The sultan of Youri advised your countrymen to proceed the remainder of the way on land, as the passage by water was rendered dangerous by numerous sunken rocks in the Niger, and a cruel race of people inhabiting the towns on its banks. They refused, however, to accede to this, observing, that they were bound to proceed down the Niger to the salt water. The old mallam further observed, that, 'as soon as the sultan of Youri heard of their death, he was much affected; but it was out of his power to punish the people who had driven them into the water. A pestilence reaching B. at the time, swept off the king and most of the inhabitants, particularly 1 those who were concerned in the transaction. The remainder, fancying it was a judgment of the white man's God, placed everything belonging to the Christians in a hut, and set it on fire.' It is said to be now a common saying, all through the interior of Africa, 'Do not hurt a Christian, for if you do, you will die like the people of Boussa."

BOUSSAC, an arrondissement, canton, and commune of France, in the dep. of La Creuse.-The arrond. has an area of 96,868 hectares, and comprises the cant. of B., Chambon, Chatelus, and Jarnages. Pop. in 1836, 37,918; in 1846, 38,833.—The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,185.—The town is situated at the confluence of the Beyroux and the Petite Creuse, 24 m. NE of Gueret. Pop. in 1789, 625; in 1836, 952. It is an ancient town, surrounded with walls, and possessing a strong castle. Its trade is chiefly in skins, leather, and cattle.
BOUSSAC (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Pleine-Fougeres. Pop. 2,679.
BOUSSAC-BOURG, a commune of France, in the dep. of Creuse, cant. of Boussac. Pop. 1,675.
BOUSSAGUES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Bedarieux. Pop. 1,138. Coal is mined in the environs.
BOUSSANGE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Moselle, com. of Gandrange. Pop. 167.
BOUSSAY-SUR-SEVRE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, 24 m. SW of Nantes. Pop. 1,799.
BOUSSENAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ariege, cant. of Massat. Pop. 2,682.
BOUSSIERES, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, arrond. of Besançon.-The cant. comprises 21 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,709.—The com. is 8 m. from Besançon. Pop. 302.
BOUSSU, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault. arrond. and 8 m. W of Mons, on the Haine. Pop. in 1835, 2,984. Coal is extensively wrought in this com.
BOUSTEAD - HILL, a township in the p. of Otho near this place in 1214. Burgh-by-Sands, Cumberland. Pop. 74.
BOUTAN. See BUTAN.
BOUTANCOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, cant. of Flize. Pop. 291.
BOUT-DE-LANDE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Guichen. Pop. 944.
BOUTEILLE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Vervins. Pop. 1,044.

BOUTON, or Buton, an island in the Molucca sea, near the SE coast of Celebes, between the parallels of 4° and 6° S; separated by a narrow strait from the island of Mounam or Pangesani. It is about 85 m. in length from N to S; and from 20 to 30 m. in breadth. Part of the surface is mountainous, and part interspersed with low hills of beautiful appearance. Fruits and vegetables are abundant; and a small part of the island is well-cultivated. Buffaloes run wild in large herds; and there are deer and wild boars in the woods. Little is known either of the island, or its inhabitants, who are of tawny complexion, short stature, and very ugly. They speak the Malay language on the coast; and profess Mahommedanism. The island is said to be wellpeopled; but the natives bear the character of being in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Chagny. Pop. treacherous. B. is governed by a sultan, whose do- 200. minion extends over the neighbouring island of Pangesani, and some others. His residence is in a fort in the town of B., the cap. of the island. It occupies the top of a steep declivity on the NW coast, near the entrance of the straits, and is surrounded with thick walls. It is of considerable size; the streets are straight and narrow; and the houses, | France, in the dep. of Moselle, arrond. of Thionville. BOU built of bamboo, and covered with palm-leaves, consist of one story. The inhabitants are industrious, and fabricate a quantity of cotton cloth, some of which is of fine texture, and bears a high price; for the cotton of B. is superior in quality to any other grown in the Eastern archipelago. The Dutch formerly had a factory here. When Stavorinus visited the island in 1773, the king of B. was in alliance with the Dutch East India company, who paid him a salary of 150 rix-dollars, or £32 14s. 6d., upon condition of his permitting an officer of the company to go over the island annually, and extirpate all the clove-trees he found upon it.

BOUTONNE, a river of France, an affluent of the Charente, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure. It rises near Chef-Boutonne, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, flows past Saint-Jean-d'Angely and Tonnay-Boutonne, and joins the Charente on the r. bank, after a course of 65 m., of which 25 m. are navigable. The commerce carried on upon it consists in wine, brandy, wood, salt, and charcoal.
BOUTRY, or BAUTRY, a Dutch factory on the Gold coast of Africa, 3 m. NE of Dixcove.
BOUVANTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Drome, cant. of Saint-Jean-en-Royans, near the Lionne. Pop. 955.

BOUVIGNES, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, arrond. of Dinant, on the 1. bank of the Meuse, 2 m. NW of Dinant. Pop. 772. Black marble is quarried in this com., and the town contains pottery-works and an iron-foundry.—B. has a place in history as the spot where Louis XI. signed a treaty with his turbulent vassal Charles of Burgundy, in 1474. In 1554 the French took the town by storm.
BOUVIGNIES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Marchiennes. Pop. 1,836.
BOUVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Pavilly. Pop. 1,107.
BOUVINES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Cisoing, 7 m. SE of Lisle. Pop. 521. Philip Augustus defeated the Emperor
BOUVRON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Blain. Pop. 2,305.
BOUX-AUX-BOIS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, cant. of Le Chene. Pop. 565.
BOUXIERES-AUX-CHENES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Nancy. Pop. 1,100.

BOUXVILLER, or Boswiller, [Germ. Buchswiller, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, arrond. of Saverne. - The cant., comprising 21 com., had a pop. of 16,792 in 1831.— The com. and town are 8 m. NE of Saverne, on the Moden. Pop. 3,756. There are extensive chemical works here, producing vitriol, alum, Prussian blue, sal ammoniae, animal charcoal, pyroligneous acid, &c.; and large tanneries and bleaching-works.
BOUZAS, a town of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. and 2 m. W of Vigo, on the bay of Vigo.
BOUZEOU, a town of Wallachia, on a river of the same name, an affluent of the Sereth, 65 m. NE of Bucharest. Pop. 2,000.
BOUZERON, a commune and village of France,
BOUZIGUES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Meze. Pop. 1,238.
BOUZILLE', a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Champtoceaux. Pop. 1,676.
BOUZONVILLE, a canton, commune, and town of —The cant., comprising 31 com., had a pop. of 18,887 in 1831.—The com. is 20 m. ESE of Thionville, on the Nied. Pop. 2,325.
BOUZOULOUK, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Orenburg, 90 m. W of Orenburg, on the l. bank of a river of the same name, an affluent of the Khoper. Pop. 2,000.
BOUZY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Marne, cant. of Ay, 16 m. SSE of Reims. Pop. 283. Fine red wines are made here.

BOVA, a town of Naples, in Calabria Ultra 1ma, in the district and 18 m. ESE of Reggio, near the coast. It has the title of a county, and is the see of a bishop. Pop. 2,882. It was greatly injured by an earthquake in 1783.
BOVEGNO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. of Brescia, on the Mella. Pop. 1,500.
BOVENDEN, a town of Hanover, in the prov. and 4 m. N of Göttingen, near the Leine. Pop. 1,800. It was formerly the cap. of the county of Plesse.
BOVENEY (UPPER and Lower), two chapelries in the p. of Burnham, in Bucks; the former with a pop. of 173; the latter, of 189.
BOVENSEE. See Bogensee.
BOVERET, a hamlet and small port in the cant. of Valais, in Switzerland, at the influx of the Rhone into the lake of Geneva.
BOVERIDGE, a hamlet in the p. of Cranbourne, Dorset. Pop. 174.
BOVERNIER, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Valais, on the Saint Bernard road, at an alt. of 708 metres. Pop. 328.

BOVES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Sains, on the Noye. Pop. 1,568.—Also a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 4 m. S of Cuneo. Pop. 7,000. Iron and marble are mined in the vicinity.
BOVEVAGH, a parish in co. Londonderry, 41 m. NNW of Dungiven. Area 19,636 acres. Pop. 5,174.
BOVEY, a town in St. Genevieve co., in the state of Missouri, U.S. Pop. 462.
BOVEY (NORTH), a parish in Devonshire, 13 m. WSW of Exeter. Area 6,600 acres. Pop. 660.
BOVEY-TRACEY, a parish in Devonshire, 4 m. SW of Chudleigh, on the E bank of the Wrey. Area 6,480 acres. Pop. 1,823. Coal and antimony are wrought here.
BOVIGNY, a commune and village of Belgium, in Luxemburg, 30 m. NNE of Neufchateau. Pop. 1,024.
BOVINA, a township in Delaware co., in the state of New York, U. S., 76 m. SSW of Albany. Pop. 1,403.—Also a v. in Warren co., in Missouri.
BOVINGDON, a chapelry in the p. of Hemel- Hempstead, 4 m. WNW of King's Langley. Area 4,160 acres. Pop. 1,072.

BOVINO, a town of Naples, in the Capitanata, 21 m. SSW of Foggia, on the Cervaro. Pop. 5,721. It is the see of a bishop, a suffragan of Benevento. The Spaniards were defeated by the Imperialists in the neighbourhood of this town in 1734.
BOVISIO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. and 11 m. N of Milan, on the Seveso. Pop. 1,000.

BOW, or Stratford-le-Bow, a parish and village in the co. of Middlesex, 4 m. ENE of St. Paul's, London, intersected by the Eastern Counties railway. Area 630 acres. Pop. 4,626. The river Lea was crossed in this p. by a narrow bridge of 3 arches, erected in the early part of the 11th cent., for which a bridge of one oblique arch of 70 ft. span was substituted in 1839.—Also a p. in Devonshire, 14 m. NW of Exeter. Area 2,740 acres. Pop. 973.

BOW, a township in Merrimac co., in the state of New Hampshire, U. S., 7 m. S of Concord, at the influx of the Turkey river into the Merrimac. Pop. 1,000. The falls in the Merrimac at this place are avoided by a canal 3 m. long, with 4 locks.
BOW, or Boa, the largest island in Lower Lough Erne, co. Fermanagh. It extends in a belt of watergirt land E and W along the foot of the lake; and measures 3½ m. in length, and 1,400 acres in area.

BOW, an island in the S Pacific, towards the eastern extremity of the group of the Society isles, in S lat. 18° 23' [Cook], 18° 04' [Beechey.] It was discovered by Bougainville in 1768. It lies low; is about 30 m. in length, and at an average 5 m. in breadth; and is shaped like a bow. The arch and cord are land; the space between them is water; the island being in fact merely a ring of land around a great lagune, which is studded with coral knolls. The cord is a flat beach, not above 300 yards broad, and so low that the sea, in some places, washes into the central lagune. It is entirely destitute of vegetation, exhibiting nothing but heaps of sea-weed lying in ridges as they are left by the tide. Two large tufts of cocoanut trees were seen at the horns or extremities of the island; and most of the arch was covered by trees, though some parts were bare and low. No bottom was found half-way along the beach, with a line of 130 fath. The pop. does not exceed 100. BOW, [Span. Arco,] an island in the Amazon river, off Cape Magoary, on the island of Marajo. It is uninhabited, but overrun with wild hogs.
BOWCOMBE, a hamlet in the p. of Carisbrooke, in the isle of Wight. Pop. 93.
